description: 7th Annual BESS Symposium Date: Monday, March 16, 2026Time: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PMLocation: Biomedical Engineering Building Join us for the 7th Annual BESS Symposium—a full day celebrating biomedical engineering research at Rutgers! Keynote Speaker:We're excited to welcome Jason Maikos, PhD, Director of VISN 2 Biomechanics Research for the Advancement of Veteran Outcomes Laboratory and BME associate member/lecturer, as our keynote speaker. Who Will Be Presenting?All BME-affiliated undergraduate, graduate, and postdoctoral students are showcasing their research through poster or oral presentations. The symposium is open to the entire BME department, including non-BME students conducting research in BME labs. Presentation Formats:  Poster Presentations: Share your research visually and engage with attendees Oral Presentations: Deliver a podium presentation to the community  Competition & Prizes:A panel of judges will evaluate all posters and oral presentations, and prizes will be awarded to the top presenters in each category! Plus: Lunch will be provided for all attendees. Whether you're presenting or attending, this is a fantastic opportunity to learn about cutting-edge research, network with peers and faculty, and celebrate the innovation happening across our department. We look forward to seeing you there!
name: 7th Annual BESS Symposium

description: CLICK HERE TO REGISTER!   The Rutgers University Honors College is thrilled to present the 1st Annual Heart & Sole 5K at Buccleuch Park!   The race kicks off at 10:30 AM sharp, so come early to grab your bib and free event t-shirt! Pickup opens at 9:00 AM at the Large Pavilion. Every registrant will also earn a finisher medal as they cross the line!   Stick around afterward for our 11:30 AM Award Ceremony, where top finishers will be recognized with exciting awards. Extra prizes will also be raffled off!   All registration fees and donations will support the American Heart Association in honor of American Heart Month. Thank you for helping us make a meaningful impact! Registration is capped at 200 participants, so don’t wait—spots will fill fast!   Additionally, Honors College students will be awarded 2 community service hours upon request!
name: Heart & Sole 5K

description: Join the Center for Women in the Arts at Douglass for a workshop led by acclaimed artist Liz Collins! This unique needlepoint workshop invites you to explore the meditative joy of stitching. Collins will guide participants through the myriad of stitches possible within needlepoint and share her distinctive methodology for creating improvisational designs, treating needlepoint as a slow drawing process in response to the grid. All skill levels and forms of stitching are welcome and supported.  Only open to Douglass and Art & Design students. Free supplies and snacks provided! Supplies/snacks are limited – First come, first served. Space is limited. Please arrive on-time. This experience counts towards the Douglass Discovery curriculum requirement. R3: Career/Discovery/Professional Development/Research. Reminder: To receive DRC curriculum credit, you must complete a reflection survey. *Liz Collins is the 2025-2026 Estelle Lebowitz & Ann Beneduce-Lebowitz Artist-in-Residence. Her solo exhibition, Shapeshifter, is on view through March 13, 2026 in the Mary H. Dana Women Artists Series Galleries at Douglass Libraries. ___ * Image and Media ReleaseBy attending this event, you acknowledge and agree that Rutgers University may photograph, record, or otherwise capture your artwork, image, and/or voice. You grant Rutgers the unrestricted right to use, reproduce, publish, and distribute these sound, still, or moving images in any medium for educational, promotional, advertising, or other purposes aligned with the University’s mission. All rights to these materials will belong to Rutgers University. Please let our photographer or videographer know if you do not want your image taken at the event.
name: Needlepoint Workshop with artist Liz Collins

description: For our Body Positivity & Self-Love Project, SJE invites you to join us for a workshop and discussion on how fatphobia shows up in our work and classroom spaces. Hosted by Dr. Joan Collier, this session will describe the experiences of plus-size students and employees on college and university campuses. Participants will reflect on ways fatphobia is rooted in every-day practices and procedures, and how the physical structures of campuses can be uncomfortable for fat bodies. To close, Dr. Collier will challenge students and colleagues to develop strategies to combat fatphobia in their personal lives and practices, and in public spaces, especially ones where leadership roles are held.    RSVP REQUIRED: GO.RUTGERS.EDU/BPSLPWORKSHOP   Facilitator: Dr. Joan Collier (she/her) Roles: Assistant Vice President for Learning and Engagement, University Academic Affairs, and Affiliate Faculty–PhD in Higher Education, Graduate School of Education
name: Navigating and Addressing Fatphobia in Our Spaces
organizationName: Center for Social Justice Education and LGBT Communities (Rutgers University)

description: To close out Body Positivity & Self-Love Project, our amazing student leaders at SJE invite you to join them for a discussion led by Sam Jones (she/her), graduate student in the College Student Affairs program, on the prevalence and impact of fatphobia in the queer community. While queer spaces are often imagined as liberating, many people, unfortunately, still experience body policing and shaming. For this dialogue, students will explore how fatphobia has catalyzed the creation of queer sub-communities, impacts self-esteem and body dysphoria, and influences relationship and community building.   Facilitator: Sam Jones (she/her) Roles: Ed.M. in College Student Affairs '26, Graduate Coordinator | LEAD Up Academy & Experiential Education | Student Involvement & Leadership
location: Center for Social Justice Education & LGBT Communities
name: Fatphobia in the Queer Community: A Student-Led Dialogue
organizationName: Center for Social Justice Education and LGBT Communities (Rutgers University)

description: Emil Ferris's critically acclaimed 2017 graphic novel, My Favorite Thing is Monsters, is a meditation on the raced and gendered instability of the gaze and its object. Its queer Chicana protagonist "feels seen" in both popular and "high" art that never imagine her as a viewer. Disidentification cannot entirely account for Ferris's romantic articulation of how underrepresented subject can feel at home in representational worlds that do not imagine them.
name: Redrawing the Western Canon: Comics, "High Art," And Revisiting the Logic of the Gaze
organizationName: Graduate Association of Interest Groups in English

description: As a graduate student, teaching assistant, instructor, or friend, you may wonder about academic accommodations and accessibility. The Office of Disability Services is hosting a drop-in morning for graduate students interested in learning about the services that ODS provides. Come stop by the Graduate Student Lounge or join via Zoom and meet Coordinator Nychey Michel to discuss accommodations for: hearing, vision, and mobility conditions, learning disabilities, psychological diagnoses (such as anxiety or depression), chronic illnesses, traumatic brain injuries, ADHD, Autism Spectrum Disorder, and more. Bring yourself or bring a friend to ask questions and learn about outreach efforts. Coffee and donuts will be provided while supplies last!   Questions or accommodation requests specific to this event? Please email dsoffice@echo.rutgers.edu, call 848-202-3111, or visit ODS in Lucy Stone Hall, Suite A145, Livingston Campus.
name: Graduate Student Disability Services Drop-In Hours

description: This co-organized event between Rutgers and Princeton University marks the inaugural meeting of scholars from both institutions focusing on Abya Yala, the Kuna term for the indigenous territory that encompasses what is now known as Latin America.  Taking up the notion of “trans-indigenous” methodologies as proposed by Chadwick Allen – that is, thinking about the indigenous beyond the imposition of colonial borders– our gathering explores connections across indigenous communities throughout the Americas, and invites scholars from across the region to participate in this conversation.  With an interdisciplinary focus, the event brings together topics such as indigenous literature, film, ancestral medicinal practices, and oral testimony to examine how indigenous knowledge systems and traditions circulate and resonate across borders, languages, and communities. We will be joined by guest keynote speaker, poet and professor Odi González (NYU), who will address the complex process of translating oral Quechua literature into written languages, such as Spanish and English. The event will finish with a film screening of Javier Corcuera's Sigo siendo (2013), a documentary which dives into the heterogenous musical expressions of Peru, influenced by local cultures and repertoires from both indigenous and Afro-Peruvian roots. With a focus on migrant subjects in the industrial capital, the film highlights the impossibility of conceiving contemporary indigenous societies in isolation, celebrating the diverse community ties they create as they work to maintain their cultural vibrancy.  Trans-Indigenous Abiayala  1st Dialogue in Indigenous Studies at Rutgers-Princeton University  April 10th Presentations 2:00 a 3:45 pm   Oriele Benavides, Princeton - "Un brusco deseo de volver”: Ficciones contrafundacionales en Piñén y El territorio del viaje de Daniela Catrileo" Gastón Pérez, Princeton - “Vectorizar el Mapudungun: peligros, oportunidades y soberanía en las inteligencias artificiales indígenas” Isabel Hackett, Princeton - From José María Arguedas to the Peruvian Truth Commission: An Acoustic Paradigm for Indigenous Rights  Estela Imigo, Princeton - Sanación, imagen y palabra: articulaciones entre el cine medicina y literatura mapuche en la obra de Francisco Huichaqueo Paul Montjoy Forti, Rutgers - “Humanidades aplicadas: Creación del Consejo Intercultural Indígena de Sabiduría Ancestral para la Conservación de los Microbiomas y la Salud- CISAM SALUD” María Chacón Rendon, Rutgers - “La cuestión indígena en la prensa nacionalista boliviana pos-Chaco: La Calle y la interpretación del indio.”    Coffee Break: Café 3:45 a 4:00   Main speaker talk  4:00 a 5:00    Odi Gonzales, NYU Professor  Translation of Oral Languages and Cultures: The Collision Between Two Worlds.  Film exhibition 5:30 pm Sigo siendo (Kackaniraqmi) (2013) by Javier Corcuera Cierre 7:30 pm
name: Trans-Indigenous Abya Yala  1st Dialogue in Indigenous Studies at Rutgers-Princeton University
organizationName: Literary Studies in Spanish Organization (Graduate Organization)

description: Join Rutgers Center for Nonprofit Leadership and Development and Rutgers SPAA Office of Community Engagement for Reimagining Democracy: Justice, Reentry & Community Voice Through Film, an evening of film and dialogue exploring issues of incarceration, reentry, and community voice. The program will feature a screening of the documentary “A Woman on the Outside,” followed by a moderated discussion with the film’s producer, Kiara C. Jones, and representatives from New Jersey Scholarship and Transformative Education in Prisons (NJ STEP). Together, we will reflect on the role of storytelling, lived experience, and education in shaping more just and inclusive communities. This event is part of Rutgers–Newark’s Solving Grand Challenges Month and is presented in partnership with the Rutgers Democracy Lab.   Please click here to register

description: Want to get more confident speaking in class, working in teams, or handling disagreements without things getting awkward? Join Improv for Democracy — a fun, interactive workshop where you’ll practice real communication skills through improv (no acting experience needed).   🗓 Friday, April 17 | 1:15–2:30 PM📍 Labor Education Center Auditorium🍕 + 🥗 Free lunch available at 12:30 pm REGISTER NOW! Space is limited.   You’ll learn how to: 🎯 Speak up without overthinking🎯 Handle different opinions with confidence🎯 Think on your feet in discussions and group work🎯 Communicate more effectively (great for interviews too) ✨ Great experiential learning activity for resumes and leadership development.   No prep. No pressure. Just show up and participate. Bring a friend — it’ll be fun, practical, and way more interactive than a typical lecture. REGISTER NOW! Space is limited.   Co-sponsored by: School of Management and Labor Relations, The Institute of Inclusive Pedagogy, the Edward J. Bloustein School of Planning and Public Policy, the School of Social Work, the Mason Gross School of Arts, the Graduate School of Education, Rutgers Business School, and the School of Communication and Information. The event is supported by Rutgers Democracy Lab and is a part of the Solving Grand Challenges Month.
name: Improv for Democracy
organizationName: School of Management and Labor Relations
organizationNames: Edward J. Bloustein School of Planning and Public Policy (Department at Rutgers)
School of Management and Labor Relations
Rutgers Graduate School of Education (University Department)

description: Have you considered exploring the Legal profession? Join the BOLD Center on Friday, April 17th for a trip to Rutgers Law School - Newark! description: The First Annual Conference: Language at Crossroads invites scholars, graduate students, and researchers to contribute to an interdisciplinary dialogue on language across its multiple levels of analysis. The conference will feature four panels: panel 1: phonetics and phonology, panel 2: morphology and syntax, panel 3: semantics and pragmatics, and panel 4: project launch. Each panel is open to contributions engaging with these levels of language, broadly conceived. Thus, contributions to neurolinguistics, cognitive sciences, psychology, psycholinguistics, sociolinguistics, discourse analysis, education, cultural studies, and related fields. Abstract Submission: here by March 20th Register: here by April 3rd
name: 1st Annual Conference: Language at Crossroads

description: Spring is here.  Join the Rutgers Global Student Volunteer Corps for a fun evening of flower making.  Our student leaders will teach attendees how to create beautiful flowers out of paper, pipe cleaners, and other materials.  By the end of the evening, you will leave with a small bouquet of realistic looking flowers to enjoy on your own or share with someone else.
name: Spring Blooms: Craft Night

description: Please join us on April 18, 2026 for the Rutgers Tree Planting Festival, where we will be planting 3000 trees using the Miyawaki Method in place of an abandoned roadway that was removed last year by Rutgers Institutional Planning and Operations. RSVP HERE!! --> https://rutgers.ca1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_41L7gvHGcte3Q2y  The goal of this event is to build community by collectively contributing to the environment and giving students first-hand experience in tree planting. There will be trees, art, and music!  This event is open to all members of the Rutgers community!  Sign up for the 10:00am-12:00pm, 12:00pm-2:00pm, or 2:00pm-5:00pm. (But you can come and go as you please!)
name: RU Tree Planting Festival
organizationNames: Forestry Club (Rutgers University)

description: Drop-in for an Artful Saturday monthly event hosted by the Center for the Women in the Arts at Douglass and Scarlet Arts RX! Join us for a Spring painting picnic in Jameson courtyard (rain location: Ludwig Center). Artsy door prizes! Bring a friend and a blanket to sit on (if you have one)! Taught by a Scarlet Arts Rx student teaching artist from Rutgers Mason Gross School of the Arts. Free supplies, snacks, and boba provided! Supplies are limited – First come, first served. All skill levels and walk-ins welcome! Foundational art skills you will learn at this event: Painting and referencing nature, Color theory and color relationships. This experience counts towards the Douglass Discovery curriculum requirement. R3: Career/Discovery/Professional Development/Research.Reminder: To receive DRC curriculum credit, you must complete a reflection survey. ___ * Image and Media Release By attending this event, you acknowledge and agree that Rutgers University may photograph, record, or otherwise capture your artwork, image, and/or voice. You grant Rutgers the unrestricted right to use, reproduce, publish, and distribute these sound, still, or moving images in any medium for educational, promotional, advertising, or other purposes aligned with the University’s mission. All rights to these materials will belong to Rutgers University. Please let our photographer or videographer know if you do not want your image taken at the event.
location: Jameson Courtyard / Schonberg Rm, Ludwig (rain location)

description: Come join us on Sunday, April 19, for Haru's biggest event of the semester, Spring 2026 Showcase! Our goal is to share Korean pop culture through the art of dance. This event is open to everyone: general members, families, Rutgers students, and non-Rutgers students! Our showcase is our biggest event, where our members get to showcase a multitude of K-pop performances they’ve been preparing for months for our audience. There are two acts with an intermission, which includes a raffle with a bunch of prizes our audience can partake in and possibly win big! And the best part is that admission is FREE for all!  You don't have to be performing to attend, so come out and support our performers! ♡ Let's cheer each other on and enjoy each other's company! You may also get some sweet treats, snacks, and refreshments from us to enjoy while watching performances 👀 Our showcase is also livestreamed on our YouTube page for audience members who cannot attend in person, but would love to support our performers! If you're watching from online, thank you for supporting Haru Dance ♡ More information on parking will be detailed here later in the semester. Contact us directly on IG: @harudance_official or Email: haru.kdcc@gmail.com if you have any questions! Can't wait to see y'all soon!
location: Cook/Douglass - Trayes Hall Douglass Student Center
name: HARU Spring 2026 Showcase

description: Solving Grand Challenges Month: Join us for a one-day workshop that explores the tension between democratic participation and scientific complexity in climate and sustainability governance. Learn from a panel of researchers and practitioners and explore methods to integrate public expert knowledges including participatory scenario planning and environmental competency groups.   Please click here to register
name: Overcoming Tensions in Climate and Sustainability Governance: A Workshop on Participatory Tools for Improving Local Democratic Processes for Sustainability
